attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic for a routine morphology scan at 20 weeks gestation. This is her second pregnancy. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy supplements. Her blood group is Rhesus negative.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The ultrasound reveals generalised fetal skin oedema, ascites, and a small pericardial effusion. The middle cerebral artery Doppler shows a peak systolic velocity of 1.7 multiples of the median. Fetal cardiac anatomy appears normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's ultrasound demonstrates classic features of fetal hydrops, which is defined as abnormal fluid accumulation in two or more fetal compartments. In this case, there is evidence of skin oedema, ascites, and pericardial effusion. The elevated middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity suggests fetal anaemia, which given the mother's Rhesus negative status, raises the possibility of Rhesus isoimmunisation as the underlying cause.

Fetal hydrops can be immune (most commonly due to red cell alloimmunisation) or non-immune (due to structural, genetic, or infectious causes). The presence of multiple fluid collections is diagnostic, and urgent referral to a maternal-fetal medicine specialist is required.

Fetal cardiac anomaly can cause hydrops but has been excluded by the normal cardiac anatomy on ultrasound. While cardiac failure may be a consequence of severe fetal anaemia, it is not the primary diagnosis in this case.

Diaphragmatic hernia typically presents with mediastinal shift and displacement of abdominal contents into the chest cavity. While it can be associated with hydrops, these characteristic findings are not described in the ultrasound.

Potter sequence is characterised by renal agenesis leading to oligohydramnios and pulmonary hypoplasia. It does not cause multiple fetal fluid collections as seen in this case.

Chromosomal anomaly such as Turner syndrome or trisomy 21 can cause hydrops, but given the normal cardiac anatomy and presence of fetal anaemia in a Rhesus negative mother, immune hydrops is more likely.
learningPoints: Fetal hydrops is characterised by abnormal fluid accumulation in two or more fetal compartments including ascites, pleural effusion, pericardial effusion, and skin oedema.
Rhesus isoimmunisation is a significant cause of fetal hydrops, but other causes include structural cardiac anomalies, chromosomal abnormalities, and infections.
Middle cerebral artery Doppler peak systolic velocity is used to assess for fetal anaemia in at-risk pregnancies.
references: Vanaparthy R, Vadakekut ES, Mahdy H. Nonimmune Hydrops Fetalis. [Updated 2024 Aug 11]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK563214/#

Younge, T., Ottolini, K. M., Al-Kouatly, H. B., & Berger, S. I. (2023). Hydrops fetalis: Incidence, Etiologies, Management Strategies, and Outcomes. Research and Reports in Neonatology, 13, 81–92. https://doi.org/10.2147/RRN.S411736
